summary: O'Neil examines a Financial Times calculator that reveals how much advertisers will pay for your personal data based on various attributes. She highlights how certain conditions like obesity or being a new parent dramatically increase your data's value, from fractions of a cent to nearly 40 cents. The post exposes how data warehouses profit from knowing intimate details about people's lives, particularly their vulnerabilities. She connects this data marketplace to broader concerns about unregulated uses of personal information, including bypassing credit protection laws.
key_points: Personal data has wildly varying market value depending on exploitable attributes like health conditions or life transitions
Being obese increases your data value nearly 400-fold because of the predatory weight-loss marketing industry
Data brokers like Acxiom assume baseline knowledge of age, gender, education, and ethnicity by default
This personal data marketplace enables companies to build unregulated scoring systems that bypass consumer protection laws
money_quotes: You can bump up your worth to $0.27 from the standard $0.0007 if you're obese, and another $0.10 if you admit to being the type to buy weight-loss products.
Data warehouses can only get that much money for your data if they know about your weight, which they may or may not since if you don't buy weight-loss products.
Some of the default assumptions are that it knows my age, gender, education level, and ethnicity.
Plenty of assumed information to, say, build an unregulated version of a credit score to bypass the Equal Credit Opportunities Act.
key_insight: Your personal data is worth more to advertisers when you're vulnerable, turning your struggles into profit opportunities for data brokers and predatory marketers.
